projects.title: Ford 2N Tractor Restoration
projects.summary: Bringing my great-grandpa's Ford 2N back to life — hauling it home, tearing it down, and restoring a piece of family history.
projects.heroAlt: Ford 2N tractor on a trailer the day we brought it home
projects.body:
This one's personal. The Ford 2N belonged to my great-grandpa, and getting it running again is a long-running project on the channel — pickup day, teardown, and every fix in between.

Notes

  • The pickup video (embedded above) covers the trailer load-out and the first walk-around once it was home.
  • The full series follows the restoration step by step — engine, electrical, sheet metal, paint.

Tips

  • Old 6-volt systems can fool you. Check grounds before you replace anything expensive.
  • If you're hauling an old tractor, chain it down at four points, not two. Ask me how I know.

Watch the full restoration playlist for the rest of the journey.

projects.title: 3D Printing for Beginners
projects.summary: Everything I wish I'd known when I started: picking a printer, first prints, slicer settings, and the mistakes that taught me the most.
projects.heroAlt: HiggProjects shop where the 3D printers live
projects.body:
3D printing has a steep first hour and a flat curve after that. This series walks through the absolute basics so a brand-new printer doesn't end up in the closet.

What's covered

  • Unboxing and first calibration.
  • Slicer walkthrough — what every setting actually does.
  • Bed adhesion: when to use glue, when to use tape, when to clean the plate.
  • Reading a failed print so you know what to change next time.

Gotchas

  • Don't chase exotic filaments before you can reliably print PLA.
  • The cheapest printer isn't always the cheapest printer once you count failed prints.

Watch the playlist below for the full beginner series.

projects.title: Craftsman Riding Mower
projects.summary: Fixing up my dad's old Craftsman riding mower — diagnosing what's broken, swapping parts, and getting it cutting grass again.
projects.heroAlt: The shop where the Craftsman mower repair series happens
projects.body:
An old riding mower is a great teacher. There's nothing on it that can't be fixed by a determined person with a socket set and the internet.

What's in the series

  • Initial diagnosis — what runs, what doesn't, what's just dirty.
  • Carburetor cleanup and small-engine basics.
  • Belt and deck work — the stuff most people pay a shop for.

Tips

  • Old fuel is the #1 culprit. Drain it before you replace anything.
  • Take pictures before you take anything apart. Future-you will thank past-you.

Full repair playlist linked below.

projects.title: Boost Your Air Compressor
projects.summary: Adding an old propane tank as an extra reservoir to a small air compressor — more capacity, longer runs, no need to buy a bigger unit.
projects.heroAlt: Small air compressor plumbed into an old propane tank for extra capacity
projects.body:
If your shop compressor runs constantly, the problem isn't always the compressor — sometimes it's just the tank size. A repurposed propane tank gives you a much bigger reservoir on the cheap.

How it works

  • Purge the propane tank thoroughly. This is non-negotiable.
  • Plumb the compressor's output into the tank with appropriate fittings and a check valve.
  • Tee off your air line from the larger combined volume.

Gotchas

  • Pressure rating matters. Verify the propane tank's working pressure before you trust it with shop air.
  • Drain water from the bottom regularly — bigger tank means more condensation collects in it.

Full walk-through in the embedded video.

projects.title: Move a Shed With a 4-Wheeler
projects.summary: Relocating a shed across the property with nothing but a 4-wheeler, some pipes, and stubbornness. Spoiler: it works better than you'd think.
projects.heroAlt: Shed being rolled across the yard on pipes pulled by a 4-wheeler
projects.body:
Renting a shed mover is expensive. Borrowing some pipe and using the 4-wheeler you already have is not. Here's how the move actually went.

The setup

  • Lift one corner at a time and slide pipe rollers underneath.
  • Strap to the 4-wheeler with a slow, steady pull — no jerks.
  • Spotter at the back, leapfrogging pipes from the back to the front as the shed rolls forward.

Lessons learned

  • Level matters more than power. A small slope downhill does most of the work for you.
  • Pipe diameter — go bigger than you think. Small pipes dig into the ground under load.

The full move (and the moments where I thought I'd made a terrible mistake) is in the video.
